authoritative_sources.title: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is official U.S. public cybersecurity risk-management guidance and provides recognized governance, control outcome, and risk-management terminology for software and digital-service compliance mapping.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Security, privacy, software, and digital-service obligation mapping; control taxonomy selection; source hierarchy and governance context
used_for: boundary between evidence mapping and security risk acceptance
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Constrains the lane to mapping control outcomes, evidence owners, and escalation paths without certifying security posture or accepting risk.

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 is a recognized public catalog of security and privacy controls for information systems and organizations, making it authoritative for access, audit, configuration, incident, privacy, and confidentiality control evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Control mapping, evidence checklist creation, access review, audit log, incident, configuration, privacy, and confidentiality evidence handling
used_for: security and privacy control gap handling
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires control outputs to tie obligations to named records, owners, review cadence, and exceptions rather than generic compliance status.

authoritative_sources.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-218 is official public guidance for secure software development practices and tasks, making it directly relevant to software release, vulnerability, supplier, secure-by-design, and review evidence workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Software development, release readiness, vulnerability remediation, supplier evidence, and secure development review preparation
used_for: release ticket and vulnerability remediation prerequisites
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Prevents software compliance handling from approving engineering work while requiring traceable development and remediation records.

authoritative_sources.title: Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations for Cybersecurity Risk Management
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3 is official public incident-response guidance that supports incident analysis, communication, recovery, lessons learned, evidence, and escalation expectations for digital services.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Incident evidence intake, containment and recovery status, client communication readiness, remediation tracking, and closeout evidence
used_for: incident record and communication evidence requirements
confidentiality and escalation triggers for incident details
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires incident-related compliance outputs to preserve evidence, owner routing, and confidentiality constraints without declaring incident resolution or legal posture.

authoritative_sources.title: Guide for Security-Focused Configuration Management of Information Systems
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-128 is official public guidance for configuration management planning, change control, impact analysis, monitoring, and documentation required before software or system changes are treated as ready.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Change-control evidence, release readiness, configuration baseline, impact analysis, monitoring, and rollback preparation
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires release and configuration changes to remain blocked when approvals, impact analysis, or rollback evidence are missing.

authoritative_sources.title: Protecting Personal Information: A Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC is the U.S. consumer-protection regulator for unfair or deceptive practices; this guide is an official business reference for personal-information safeguards, retention, access, vendor handling, disposal, and incident response.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Personal information handling, data minimization, access control, vendor evidence, retention, disposal, and confidentiality review
used_for: legal and privacy owner escalation boundaries
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Constrains the lane to evidence readiness and owner routing for personal information rather than privacy-law interpretation or data-use approval.

authoritative_sources.title: Secure by Design
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. cyber defense agency and its Secure by Design guidance provides official public expectations for software manufacturers, default security, vulnerability handling, customer-impact transparency, and secure product outcomes.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Product security evidence, vulnerability response, secure-by-default claims, customer assurance, and product-owner escalation
used_for: boundary between compliance support and product security ownership
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Keeps secure-by-design handling evidence-backed while routing risk acceptance, product changes, and customer commitments to owners.

authoritative_sources.title: Cloud Controls Matrix
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Cloud Security Alliance maintains a vendor-neutral cloud-control framework used by cloud and SaaS providers to organize cloud control domains, customer assurance, STAR self-assessment, and evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cloud and SaaS control mapping, customer questionnaire readiness, shared-responsibility evidence, and customer assurance packet preparation
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Prevents SaaS compliance support from claiming certification while enabling structured evidence packets for customer assurance.

authoritative_sources.title: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: W3C publishes WCAG as the recognized technical accessibility standard for web content and digital interfaces, making it authoritative for accessibility terminology, QA evidence, and conformance-support workflow.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Digital content and product accessibility QA evidence, success criterion mapping, remediation tracking, and approval boundary handling
used_for: accessibility evidence and QA artifact expectations
boundary between evidence packaging and legal accessibility conclusions
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires accessibility handling to name success criteria and evidence while escalating legal sufficiency and exception approvals.

authoritative_sources.title: 17 U.S.C. Section 512 - Limitations on liability relating to material online
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The U.S. Copyright Office publishes the public text of copyright law; Section 512 is directly relevant to online service provider takedown, counter-notice, agent, repeat-infringer, and rights-record workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Content rights, takedown notice, counter-notice, repeat-infringer, rights metadata, and legal owner escalation workflow
used_for: confidential handling of rights records and notices
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Allows content workflow evidence packaging while refusing copyright legal judgment, takedown legal strategy, or rights-holder negotiation.

authoritative_sources.title: 16 CFR Part 312 - Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The current eCFR text is the official public source for the FTC COPPA Rule, relevant when a digital product, app, site, game, or content workflow involves children or child-directed services.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Child-directed service, age-audience, consent, notice, data collection, retention, and privacy-owner escalation workflow
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires age-audience and jurisdiction facts before control conclusions and routes COPPA interpretation to legal or privacy owners.

workflow_stages.name: Information compliance intake and authority prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm tenant policy, service model, jurisdiction, product or content scope, business unit, client commitments, confidentiality constraints, source hierarchy, approval matrix, systems of record, retrieval hooks, and adjacent authority owners before work enters compliance review.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Obligation and control mapping
workflow_stages.description: Map NIST, FTC, CISA, CSA, W3C, U.S. Copyright Office, eCFR, tenant policies, platform policies, client commitments, and SOW terms to controls, evidence sources, review cadence, owners, and escalation paths.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

workflow_stages.name: Evidence truth-set assembly
workflow_stages.description: Assemble client files, engagement records, project plans, tickets, change records, release records, access reviews, audit logs, data-flow maps, secure-development records, vulnerability records, incident records, content assets, rights records, accessibility QA, approvals, communication logs, and retention locations into a traceable evidence packet.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Review readiness and signoff-boundary handling
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether visible records support a review-ready, evidence gap, stale evidence, source conflict, approval conflict, confidentiality hold, exception, remediation, or blocked state without granting legal, editorial, privacy, security, engineering, accessibility, copyright, contractual, attestation, or final compliance signoff.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Exception and remediation escalation
workflow_stages.description: Route missing records, contradictory sources, policy-versus-practice conflicts, control failures, approval blockers, incident evidence gaps, vulnerability remediation gaps, data handling conflicts, accessibility defects, content-rights issues, or confidentiality concerns to accountable owners with required remediation evidence.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Client delivery, confidentiality, and closeout evidence
workflow_stages.description: Maintain approved-recipient, approved-channel, engagement-scope, client-rights, subscriber-data, content-rights, incident-sensitivity, vulnerability-detail, credential, and retention checks before delivering review packets or marking compliance support complete.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

artifacts: Tenant information compliance policy, service model, source hierarchy, approval matrix, confidentiality constraints, quality standards, control framework scope, escalation thresholds, retention rules, and completion gates

Client files, engagement records, SOW or contract terms, product requirements, project plans, release tickets, change advisory records, incident records, service deliverables, review notes, quality checks, communication logs, and closeout packets

Data-flow maps, system inventories, access review exports, audit-log samples, secure development records, vulnerability remediation proof, supplier evidence, configuration baseline evidence, rollback plans, and monitoring records

Privacy review records, personal-information inventory, processing purpose notes, consent or preference records, data-subject or subscriber request records, vendor records, retention schedules, disposal records, and policy exception logs

Content assets, rights metadata, publication workflow records, moderation records, takedown notices, counter-notices, repeat-infringer records, accessibility testing evidence, WCAG success criterion notes, remediation records, and approved delivery records

Customer assurance materials, security questionnaire records, SOC 2 or ISO evidence packet inputs, reviewer comments, exception register, remediation plan, attestation-owner notes, and retention locations
systems_of_record: document management and client file system
content management and digital asset management system
GRC, control, policy, and evidence management system
security monitoring, vulnerability, incident, and audit-log systems
identity, access review, and privileged-access systems
source control, CI/CD, change management, release, and configuration management systems
privacy, consent, preference, data-subject request, and retention systems
rights management, takedown, accessibility testing, proofing, QA, and approval systems
client communication, secure transfer, audit log, remediation, and retention systems

allowed: Determine whether documented information compliance evidence supports moving a work item to intake accepted, evidence requested, review ready, blocked for evidence, blocked for owner review, exception, remediation, escalation, complete, or closed.

Prepare evidence-backed control-obligation matrices, evidence gap summaries, review-preparation packets, exception remediation notes, policy-conflict notes, confidentiality hold notes, and adjacent-owner handoff packages.

Layer tenant policies, platform policies, client commitments, SOW terms, and approval rules on top of public NIST, FTC, CISA, CSA, W3C, Copyright Office, and eCFR baselines while recording assumptions and source hierarchy.
escalate: The applicable jurisdiction, business unit, product, service, content collection, data category, audience, control framework, source hierarchy, tenant policy, client approval rule, or system record is missing and would change the control outcome.
Public guidance, standards, statutes, tenant policy, platform policy, client instructions, SOW terms, operational practice, and retrieved records disagree materially.

Legal interpretation, regulator-facing wording, editorial approval, privacy-law posture, security risk acceptance, engineering judgment, accessibility legal conclusion, copyright legal judgment, contractual commitment, attestation conclusion, or client rights determine the answer.
Policy and operational practice conflict on whether release, content delivery, incident closure, control closeout, evidence publication, client communication, or retention can proceed.

A confidentiality, approved-recipient, approved-channel, subscriber-data, client-rights, content-rights, incident-sensitivity, vulnerability-detail, credential, unreleased-product, or data-security conflict affects delivery or use of information.
refuse: Requests to approve legal compliance, privacy posture, security posture, engineering change, risk acceptance, editorial content, accessibility legal sufficiency, copyright position, takedown strategy, client commitment, financial approval, contractual exception, SOC 2 or ISO attestation, certification, or final client acceptance.

Requests to mark a control satisfied, release approved, incident closed, content cleared, deliverable accepted, or compliance work complete without required source context, tenant facts, system records, evidence, approvals, remediation proof, communication records, confidentiality clearance, or retention evidence.

Requests to disclose, reuse, export, train on, publish, or transmit client files, subscriber data, content assets, source extracts, credentials, vulnerability details, incident records, rights records, or confidential deliverables outside approved engagement, recipient, channel, purpose, and tenant-security rules.

domain_failure_modes: Treating a partial client file, stale control screenshot, informal chat approval, outdated system inventory, or old data-flow map as sufficient support for control readiness.

Clearing release, content delivery, incident closure, customer assurance, or compliance closeout without access-review evidence, audit-log samples, vulnerability remediation proof, privacy records, accessibility evidence, content-rights records, approval notes, or retention locations.

Presenting evidence packaging as legal advice, privacy-law interpretation, copyright judgment, accessibility legal conclusion, engineering approval, security risk acceptance, contractual signoff, certification, or attestation conclusion.

Suppressing a source conflict, policy-versus-practice conflict, child-audience flag, data-use conflict, control failure, vulnerability exception, incident communication issue, rights issue, accessibility defect, or approval gap to meet a delivery date.
Using operational practice to override tenant policy, public guidance, standards, SOW terms, client instructions, platform policy, or retrieved system evidence.

Disclosing or reusing client files, subscriber records, content assets, vulnerability details, incident data, credentials, source extracts, takedown materials, or confidential deliverables through an unapproved recipient, purpose, system, or channel.

Flattening software release, privacy, security, accessibility, copyright, records, content, incident, client delivery, and assurance workflows into generic project tasks without evidence and owner context.
Treating SOC 2, ISO, security questionnaire, customer assurance, accessibility, or copyright review preparation as authority to certify, attest, approve, or negotiate.
Confidently reconciling conflicting public guidance, statutes, standards, tenant policy, platform rules, SOW terms, client instructions, and operational practice without human owner resolution.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Client request that crosses into advisory or signoff authority
Confidentiality or approval conflict in delivery workflow
Missing evidence for a required control or obligation
Request for legal interpretation, regulator negotiation, or formal signoff
A required control is missing evidence
Client or content workflow handling with evidence and approval awareness

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction, audience, product, platform, data category, and content-rights applicability
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack uses public NIST, FTC, CISA, CSA, W3C, U.S. Copyright Office, and eCFR sources as the baseline. Tenant context must identify jurisdiction, product, audience, platform, data category, content collection, client terms, and source hierarchy before the lane can issue more than a safe hold or evidence request.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant authority for mechanical gating, release state changes, incident closure, content delivery, or control closeout
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The specification-based baseline does not grant release, mutation, incident closure, content clearance, control closeout, or client-delivery authority. A runtime may only perform mechanical gating or mutation if tenant policy explicitly grants that authority and all evidence, approval, audit, and rollback prerequisites are retrieved.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Legal sufficiency, editorial judgment, privacy posture, security risk acceptance, accessibility legal posture, copyright decisions, and attestation conclusions
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The lane treats these as escalation triggers because public sources, jurisdictional rules, standards, platform policies, tenant commitments, client instructions, and professional authority requirements can differ materially.

notes: The research set covers workflow, terminology, evidence, quality handling, confidentiality, digital service controls, software development, incident response, privacy, accessibility, content rights, and failure modes using NIST, FTC, CISA, CSA, W3C, U.S. Copyright Office, and eCFR sources.

No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains because the pack explicitly blocks or routes when jurisdiction, audience, product, data category, content rights, source hierarchy, client authority, tenant policy, confidentiality, approval, legal, editorial, privacy, security, engineering, accessibility, copyright, contractual, attestation, or regulator-facing facts are missing.
